meta.name: Hortus Ancient Growing Library
meta.description: A comprehensive library of food and herb growing wisdom drawn from ancient and medieval sources, curated for modern gardeners learning timeless techniques.

sources.title: De Agri Cultura (On Agriculture)
sources.author: Cato the Elder (Marcus Porcius Cato)
sources.description: The oldest surviving complete prose work in Latin. Practical farming manual covering soil preparation, crop rotation, composting, orchard management, and herb cultivation.

sources.title: Rerum Rusticarum (On Agriculture)
sources.author: Marcus Terentius Varro
sources.description: Systematic treatise on agriculture organized by topic: field crops, livestock, and villa farming. Scientific classification of soils and planting calendars.

sources.title: De Re Rustica (On Agriculture)
sources.author: Lucius Junius Moderatus Columella
sources.description: The most comprehensive Roman agricultural encyclopedia. 12 books covering soil science, composting, vegetables, herbs, orchards, vines, and garden design.

sources.title: Naturalis Historia (Natural History)
sources.author: Pliny the Elder
sources.description: Encyclopedic work covering botany, agriculture, and medicinal herbs. Books 17-19 cover horticulture, Books 20-27 cover medicinal plants.

sources.title: Opus Agriculturae
sources.author: Rutilius Taurus Aemilianus Palladius
sources.description: Month-by-month agricultural calendar. Each book covers tasks for a specific month — the original seasonal planting guide.

sources.title: Hortulus (The Little Garden)
sources.author: Walahfrid Strabo
sources.description: A 440-line Latin poem about the author's monastery garden at Reichenau Abbey. Describes 23 medicinal and culinary herbs with growing instructions and uses.

sources.title: Plan of Saint Gall
sources.author: Anonymous Benedictine monks
sources.description: The earliest known garden plan in Western civilization. Shows a physic garden (16 medicinal herbs), a kitchen garden (18 vegetables), and an orchard-cemetery.

sources.title: Physica (Natural History) & Causae et Curae
sources.author: Hildegard of Bingen
sources.description: Comprehensive herbal medicine and gardening treatise by a Benedictine abbess. Catalogs hundreds of plants with medicinal properties and growing guidance.

sources.title: Kitab al-Filaha (Book of Agriculture)
sources.author: Ibn al-'Awwam
sources.description: Monumental agricultural encyclopedia covering 585 plants and 50 fruit trees. Synthesizes Greek, Roman, and Islamic agricultural science.

sources.title: Five Hundred Points of Good Husbandry
sources.author: Thomas Tusser
sources.description: Versified month-by-month guide to farming and gardening. Lists herbs for the kitchen, strewing, medicine, and distillation.

sources.title: A Garden of Herbs
sources.author: Eleanour Sinclair Rohde
sources.description: Comprehensive guide to growing and using herbs, drawing on centuries of herbalist tradition from medieval monks to Tudor gardeners.

sources.title: Ancient and Medieval Gardens
sources.author: Robert Bedrosian (compiler)
sources.description: 961-page searchable compilation of scholarly sources on gardening in antiquity and the Middle Ages, including herbology, folklore, and mythology.

collection_stats.positioning: Expanded v2 starter library for new gardeners, emphasizing herbs, staple vegetables, pulses, roots, perennial herbs, and classic Mediterranean/monastic crops from ancient and medieval traditions.

traditions.name: Roman Agriculture
traditions.summary: The Romans developed systematic agriculture into a science. Key innovations include crop rotation, composting theory, raised beds, and detailed planting calendars tied to astronomical observations.
key_principles: Soil classification by type (rich, lean, wet, dry, warm, cold)
Composting and manure management as a science
Crop rotation to prevent soil exhaustion
Raised bed construction for drainage and warmth
Planting by astronomical and weather signs
Companion planting observations
Seed selection and storage techniques

traditions.name: Monastic Gardening
traditions.summary: Monasteries preserved and refined ancient growing knowledge through the Dark Ages. The three-garden system (physic garden, kitchen garden, orchard) became the model for organized food production across Europe.
key_principles: Three-garden system: physic, kitchen, and orchard
Enclosed gardens for microclimate control
Medicinal herb cultivation as sacred duty
Seed saving and exchange networks between monasteries
Lunar planting calendars integrated with liturgical seasons
Record-keeping of planting dates and yields
Raised beds with careful soil amendment
Sacred geometry in garden layout

traditions.name: Islamic Golden Age Agriculture
traditions.summary: Islamic scholars synthesized Greek, Roman, Persian, and Indian agricultural knowledge, adding advanced irrigation, soil science, and the introduction of dozens of new crops to Europe via Al-Andalus.
key_principles: Advanced irrigation systems (qanat, noria, saqiya)
Soil analysis and amendment
Introduction of citrus, rice, cotton, sugarcane to Europe
Botanical classification systems
Grafting and plant breeding techniques
Crop acclimatization methods
Water conservation and distribution

traditions.name: Tudor & Stuart English Gardening
traditions.summary: The English cottage garden tradition combined monastic herb knowledge with practical food production. Herbalists like Gerard, Parkinson, and Culpeper documented centuries of accumulated wisdom.
key_principles: Kitchen garden as extension of the household
Herb categories: pot herbs, salad herbs, strewing herbs, medicinal herbs
Monthly planting calendars in verse (Tusser)
Companion planting folklore
Preservation and stillroom techniques
Cold frame and cloche usage for season extension

techniques.name: Roman Raised Bed Construction
techniques.summary: Columella describes raising garden beds above the surrounding paths for drainage, warmth, and ease of cultivation — the same technique used in modern raised bed gardening.
instructions: Mark beds no wider than a person can reach across from either side (approximately 4 feet)
Dig pathways between beds to a depth of one spade, piling soil onto the bed area
Shape beds with a gentle crown so water drains to the paths
Amend soil with well-rotted manure from the second compost pit (aged at least one year)
Border beds with planks, stones, or woven willow if the soil is sandy
techniques.modern_notes: Essentially identical to modern raised bed practice. The 4-foot width recommendation has endured for 2,000 years.

techniques.name: Roman Two-Pit Composting
techniques.summary: Columella prescribes two manure pits: one for fresh material aging for a year, and one for mature compost ready for use. The pits are lined with packed earth to retain moisture.
instructions: Dig two pits with gently sloping sides, like shallow fish ponds
Line the pits with packed clay or heavy earth to prevent moisture loss
Fill the first pit with fresh manure, straw, kitchen scraps, and garden waste
Keep the material consistently moist — never let it dry out
After one year, the compost moves conceptually to 'pit two' status — ready for the garden
Weed seeds and bramble seeds will have decayed during the year of decomposition
techniques.modern_notes: This is hot composting with a maturation period. The emphasis on moisture retention and weed seed destruction is remarkably modern.

techniques.name: Roman Crop Rotation
techniques.summary: Varro and Columella describe alternating crops to maintain soil fertility. Legumes (beans, lupines, vetch) are used to restore what grain crops deplete.
instructions: Never plant the same crop family in the same bed two years running
Follow grain crops with legumes (beans, peas, lentils, lupines) to restore the soil
Follow legumes with root vegetables or brassicas
Allow fields to lie fallow every third year if possible, or plant a green manure crop
Lupines can be turned into the soil as green manure — they enrich even poor ground
techniques.modern_notes: The Romans understood nitrogen fixation by legumes empirically, 1,900 years before the science was explained. Their rotation principles remain best practice.

techniques.name: The Monastic Physic Garden
techniques.summary: The Plan of Saint Gall shows a dedicated medicinal herb garden with 16 rectangular beds, each containing a single herb species, bordered by a path for easy access.
instructions: Locate the physic garden near the infirmary or house for easy access
Lay out rectangular beds approximately 3-4 feet wide for easy reach
Plant one herb species per bed to simplify harvest and prevent cross-contamination
Include a central path wide enough to kneel beside any bed
Surround with a wall or dense hedge to create a sheltered microclimate
The 16 herbs of Saint Gall: lily, rose, kidney bean, savory, rosemary, mint, sage, rue, iris, pennyroyal, fenugreek, cumin, lovage, fennel, tansy, and watercress
techniques.modern_notes: This organized, single-species bed approach is excellent for beginners. The wall/hedge creates a warm microclimate that extends the growing season.

techniques.name: The Monastic Kitchen Garden
techniques.summary: The Saint Gall kitchen garden contains 18 rectangular beds for vegetables, each dedicated to a single crop, arranged in a grid pattern.
instructions: Lay out 18 rectangular beds in two rows of nine
Dedicate each bed to one crop for ease of cultivation
The 18 vegetables of Saint Gall: onion, leek, celery, coriander, dill, poppy, radish, chervil, lettuce, garlic, shallot, parsley, cabbage, beet, black cumin, parsnip, corn salad, and chard
Place the garden near the kitchen for daily harvesting
Maintain clean paths between beds for access and drainage
techniques.modern_notes: This is essentially a modern potager or kitchen garden layout. Many of these crops remain staples of home gardening.

techniques.name: Walahfrid's Herb Garden Method
techniques.summary: Walahfrid Strabo describes clearing, preparing, and planting his monastery herb garden at Reichenau Abbey, including dealing with weeds, nettles, and moles.
instructions: Begin in early spring by clearing the bed of dead stalks and old growth
Pull nettles and weeds by hand, protecting hands with gloves or cloth
Break up the soil and raise the beds into mounded rows
Plant seeds or divisions in the raised mounds
Water newly planted beds with gentle rain-like sprinkling, not flooding

Walahfrid's 23 herbs: sage, rue, southernwood, gourd, melon, wormwood, horehound, fennel, iris, lovage, chervil, lily, poppy, clary sage, mint, pennyroyal, celery, betony, agrimony, tansy, catmint, radish, and rose
techniques.modern_notes: Walahfrid's poem is the earliest first-person account of hands-on gardening in Western literature. His practical, gentle approach resonates with modern organic gardening.

techniques.name: Lunar Planting Calendar
techniques.summary: Ancient and medieval gardeners planted according to moon phases. Root crops were sown during the waning moon, above-ground crops during the waxing moon.
instructions: Plant leafy greens, herbs, and above-ground crops during the waxing moon (new to full)
Plant root crops (carrots, beets, radishes, onions) during the waning moon (full to new)
Transplant and graft during the waxing moon when sap rises
Prune and harvest for storage during the waning moon
Avoid planting on the day of the new moon or full moon itself
Combine lunar timing with seasonal awareness from Palladius's monthly calendar
techniques.modern_notes: Lunar planting remains practiced worldwide. While modern science is divided on its efficacy, it provides a useful rhythm and discipline for garden planning.

techniques.name: Ancient Seed Selection & Storage
techniques.summary: Cato and Varro describe selecting the best seeds from the strongest plants and storing them properly for next season — the foundation of plant breeding.
instructions: Select seeds only from the healthiest, most vigorous plants
Allow seed heads to fully ripen and dry on the plant before harvesting
Thresh seeds and winnow to remove chaff
Store in cool, dry conditions in clay pots sealed with pitch or wax
Test germination before planting by soaking a sample — discard any that float
Exchange seeds with neighboring farms to maintain genetic diversity
techniques.modern_notes: These seed saving principles are unchanged. The emphasis on selecting from the best plants is the basis of all traditional plant breeding.

techniques.name: Water-Wise Irrigation (Islamic Tradition)
techniques.summary: Ibn al-'Awwam describes efficient irrigation methods that deliver water directly to plant roots, minimizing waste — principles that align with modern drip irrigation.
instructions: Channel water to the base of plants, not over the foliage
Water in the early morning or evening to reduce evaporation
Create shallow basins around individual trees and large plants to hold water
Use clay pots buried in the soil (ollas) to provide slow, steady moisture to roots
Mulch the soil surface to retain moisture between waterings
Observe the soil and plants for signs of water need rather than watering on a fixed schedule
techniques.modern_notes: The buried clay pot (olla) irrigation method is experiencing a revival in modern permaculture. These water conservation principles are critical for sustainable gardening.

techniques.name: Ancient Companion Planting
techniques.summary: Pliny and other Roman writers observed that certain plants thrive together while others inhibit each other. These observations form the basis of companion planting.
instructions: Plant basil near rue with caution — Pliny says they are enemies
Grow legumes (beans, peas) near crops that need rich soil — they enrich the ground
Plant aromatic herbs (rosemary, sage, thyme) near brassicas to repel pests
Keep fennel isolated — it was considered harmful to most neighboring plants
Alternate rows of tall and short plants so all receive adequate light
Grow vines and climbing beans on trellises to shade tender lettuce below
techniques.modern_notes: Many of Pliny's companion planting observations have been validated by modern research. Fennel's allelopathic properties are well documented.

techniques.name: Ancient Soil Reading & Preparation
techniques.summary: Columella teaches how to evaluate soil by taste, smell, color, and texture before planting — a sensory approach to soil science.
instructions: Taste the soil: sweet soil is fertile, bitter or salty soil needs amendment
Smell the soil: good soil has an earthy, pleasant smell after rain
Observe color: dark soil is generally richer than pale or reddish soil
Test texture: squeeze moist soil in your hand — it should hold together but crumble when poked
Dig a hole and refill it: if soil mounds above the hole, it is rich; if it sinks, it is lean
Amend lean soil with well-rotted compost, wood ash, and green manure crops
Amend heavy clay with sand and organic matter; amend sandy soil with clay and compost
techniques.modern_notes: The squeeze test and dig-and-refill test are still taught in modern soil science. Columella's sensory approach gives beginners an intuitive feel for soil quality.

techniques.name: Tusser's Monthly Garden Calendar
techniques.summary: Thomas Tusser's 1573 verse guide assigns specific gardening tasks to each month, creating a rhythm of work that carries the gardener through the year.
instructions: January: Plan the garden, order seeds, repair tools and fences
February: Prepare beds, sow early peas and beans under cover
March: Sow most herb and vegetable seeds, transplant overwinted crops
April: Succession sow salads and radishes, set out tender herbs after frost
May: Plant out warm-season crops, begin regular weeding and watering
June: Harvest early crops, sow succession plantings, gather herbs for drying
July: Continue harvest, dry herbs at their peak, begin saving seeds
August: Harvest main crops, prepare storage, sow autumn crops
September: Plant garlic and overwintering onions, harvest roots for storage
October: Clear spent crops, spread compost, plant cover crops
November: Protect tender plants, continue composting, maintain tools
December: Rest, study, plan next year's garden
techniques.modern_notes: Tusser's calendar is for English climate zones (USDA 7-8). Adjust timing forward or back based on your local frost dates. The rhythm of monthly tasks remains valuable everywhere.

plants.growing_notes: Walahfrid placed sage first in his garden, calling it the most prized herb. Thrives in well-drained, sunny positions. Cut back after flowering to maintain vigor. Propagate from cuttings or division.

ancient_uses: Medicinal: antidote to poison, eye health
plants.growing_notes: Walahfrid describes rue's blue-green fronds and heavy fragrance. Plant in dry, poor soil in full sun. An extremely hardy perennial. Handle with gloves — sap can cause skin irritation in sunlight.

ancient_uses: Culinary: seeds, fronds, and bulb all edible
plants.growing_notes: Plant fennel away from other herbs and vegetables — Pliny notes it inhibits neighboring plants. Loves full sun and well-drained soil. Self-seeds prolifically. Harvest seeds when they turn brown.

ancient_uses: Household: strewing herb for freshening rooms
plants.growing_notes: Walahfrid marvels at mint's vigor and spreading habit. Plant in containers or confined beds to prevent takeover. Prefers moist, partially shaded conditions. Harvest frequently to encourage bushy growth.

Romans grew rosemary near doorways for protection. Needs excellent drainage and full sun. Tolerates drought once established. In cold climates (below USDA Zone 7), grow in pots and overwinter indoors.

ancient_uses: Medicinal: Cato considered it a cure-all
Economic: staple crop for monastery kitchens
plants.growing_notes: Cato devoted more text to cabbage than any other plant, calling it superior to all vegetables for health. Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost. Transplant to rich, firm soil. Needs consistent moisture and cool weather.

ancient_uses: Culinary: foundation of most cooked dishes
Economic: long storage made it invaluable
plants.growing_notes: Listed first among the 18 vegetables in the Saint Gall plan. Plant sets or seeds in early spring in loose, well-drained soil. Onions are heavy feeders — amend with compost. Stop watering when tops begin to fall over.

ancient_uses: Culinary: fresh salads, a Roman delicacy
Medicinal: sleep aid (the milky sap is mildly sedative)
plants.growing_notes: Columella describes multiple varieties. Sow in succession every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest. Prefers cool weather — provide shade in summer. Harvest outer leaves for cut-and-come-again, or let heads mature.

ancient_uses: Protective: ward off disease and evil
plants.growing_notes: Plant individual cloves in autumn, pointed end up, 2 inches deep and 6 inches apart. Garlic needs a cold period to form bulbs. Harvest when lower leaves brown. Cure in a dry, airy space for 2 weeks before storage.

ancient_uses: Culinary: dried, boiled, or ground into flour
Agricultural: primary nitrogen-fixing crop for rotation
Cultural: used in Roman religious ceremonies
plants.growing_notes: The most important legume of the ancient world. Sow in autumn or very early spring — they prefer cool weather. Plants fix nitrogen, enriching the soil for the next crop. After harvest, turn the stalks into the soil as green manure.

ancient_uses: Symbolic: used in Roman funeral rites
plants.growing_notes: Notoriously slow to germinate — soak seeds overnight and be patient (2-4 weeks). Biennial: produces leaves the first year, flowers and seeds the second. Grows well in partial shade. Rich, moist soil preferred.

ancient_uses: Beekeeping: Romans planted thyme fields for honey production
plants.growing_notes: Pliny notes the finest honey comes from thyme-rich regions. Plant in full sun with excellent drainage. Tolerates poor, rocky soil — Mediterranean plants thrive on neglect. Trim after flowering. Propagate from cuttings or division.

ancient_uses: Medicinal: throat and voice (Nero reportedly ate leeks daily to strengthen his voice)
Cultural: among the most popular Roman vegetables
plants.growing_notes: Start seeds indoors in late winter. Transplant into deep holes or trenches and gradually fill soil around the stems to blanch them white. Heavy feeders — require rich, moist soil. Very cold-hardy; can overwinter in many climates.

ancient_uses: Household: laundry scenting (the name comes from Latin 'lavare', to wash)
plants.growing_notes: Romans used lavender extensively in their baths. Requires full sun and excellent drainage — add gravel to heavy soils. Prune annually after flowering, cutting to just above the woody growth. Do not overwater.

ancient_uses: Culinary: roots and greens both eaten
Agricultural: easy to grow and store
plants.growing_notes: Listed in the Saint Gall plan. Sow directly in the garden 2-4 weeks before last frost. Each 'seed' is actually a cluster — thin seedlings to 3-4 inches apart. Harvest when roots are 1-3 inches across. The greens are as nutritious as the roots.

ancient_uses: Cultural: Romans grew radishes the size of turnips
plants.growing_notes: Walahfrid includes radish among his 23 herbs. The fastest vegetable from seed to table — as little as 3-4 weeks. Sow directly every 2 weeks for continuous harvest. Prefers cool weather; bolts in heat. Thin to 2 inches apart.
Ancient kitchen and physic gardens both prized dill. Sow direct in cool weather; dislikes transplanting. Let some flower for pollinators and seed harvest.

ancient_uses: Preserving: seed spice for meats and vegetables
plants.growing_notes: A classic herb of Roman and Islamic gardens. Sow direct and succession plant because it bolts in heat. Harvest leaves young and seeds when brown.
A towering perennial from monastery gardens. Give it rich soil and room; harvest young leaves for the best flavor. Divide every few years.

ancient_uses: Culinary: delicate salad and soup herb
plants.growing_notes: A cool-season herb that prefers spring and autumn. Sow direct; heat makes it bolt fast. Excellent for new gardeners who need an easy soft herb.
One of the core old-world seasoning herbs. Sow after frost in light soil and full sun. Harvest before flowering for strongest flavor.
A hardy shrub herb for dry sunny places. Prune after bloom to keep it compact. Loved by bees and useful as a low hedge.
A classic Roman evergreen. In cold climates grow in a pot and overwinter indoors. Clip regularly to shape and harvest mature leaves.

ancient_uses: Culinary: salads and sour pot herb
plants.growing_notes: Ancient growers valued purslane as both crop and volunteer edible. Thrives in heat and poor soil. Harvest tips often to keep tender growth coming.
Romans ate chicory raw and cooked. Sow direct in spring or late summer. Harvest young leaves mild, older leaves cooked.
Best in cool wet conditions. Grow near consistently moist soil or shallow flowing water. Harvest young tops regularly.
A reliable perennial green. Grow in sun or part shade and cut leaves often. Remove flower stalks to keep leaves tender.

ancient_uses: Culinary: leaves as soft pot herb
plants.growing_notes: Ancient cooks used mallow as both medicine and food. Easy from seed and tolerant of rough conditions. Harvest young leaves for best texture.
An old staple root. Sow fresh seed directly in spring; germination is slow. Flavor improves after cold weather.
A dependable ancient staple for beginners. Sow spring and late summer. Thin early and use thinnings as greens.
Direct sow early in cool weather with support for tall varieties. Peas enrich the soil and fit beautifully into an ancient rotation plan.
A foundational ancient food crop. Best in drier climates and lean soils. Harvest when pods dry and finish drying under cover.

ancient_uses: Culinary: pulse for soups and pastes
plants.growing_notes: A major Mediterranean crop. Sow after danger of hard frost in a spot that stays fairly dry. Avoid rich soil that pushes too much leaf growth.
Old celery was often stronger and leafier than modern types. Needs steady moisture and rich soil. Start early and never let it dry out.
Ancient carrots were often purple, yellow, or white. Sow directly in fine loose soil; thin carefully. Keep evenly moist until germination.
Romans famously prized cucumbers. Plant after warm weather arrives in rich soil with regular water. Trellising saves space and improves fruit quality.
Melons need warmth, fertility, and patience. Start indoors in short-season climates and plant into the warmest bed you have.

ancient_uses: Utility: dried shells for vessels and tools
plants.growing_notes: Walahfrid wrote lovingly about gourds climbing high. Give them a strong trellis and a long warm season. Harvest young to eat or mature to dry.
An abundant crop for new gardeners. Sow after frost or transplant carefully. Needs space, compost, and regular water while establishing.
Grown in the ancient Mediterranean as a prized delicacy. Needs rich soil and full sun; best as a perennial in mild climates.
Romans loved asparagus and even developed quick-cooking sayings around it. Plant crowns in deep prepared trenches and wait patiently; the reward lasts for years.
Listed in the Saint Gall physic garden. Easy from seed in warm weather. Can be cut young for greens or grown to maturity for aromatic seed.
A warm-season spice crop from the old world. Needs heat and a longer season than dill or coriander. Harvest seed heads when dry and tan.

ancient_uses: Culinary: petals in syrups and conserves
plants.growing_notes: The Saint Gall plan included roses in the physic garden. Choose fragrant old varieties for best culinary and medicinal use. Full sun and airflow keep plants healthy.
Valued for both beauty and useful rhizomes. Plant in sun with rhizomes partly exposed. Divide clumps every few years.

ancient_uses: Culinary: used cautiously in historic dishes
plants.growing_notes: A strong old herb with vigorous growth. Best treated as a boundary or utility plant, not a main kitchen herb. Use cautiously; historical significance is greater than modern culinary use.
A major medieval medicinal herb. Grow in sun or part shade in ordinary soil. Harvest leaves before flowering.
Easy, forgiving, and useful. Grow in sun and trim after bloom to renew growth. Let some flower for pollinators.
A silver-leaved bitter herb from many old gardens. Best in dry sun and poor soil. Grow for tradition and utility more than kitchen use.

seasonal_calendar.description: A synthesis of Palladius's monthly calendar, Tusser's verse guide, and monastic planting traditions, adapted for modern gardeners. Adjust timing based on your local frost dates.

months.name: January
tasks: Study seed catalogs and plan the garden layout
Sharpen and repair tools
Turn compost piles on mild days
Order seeds from heritage/heirloom sources
Start a garden journal

months.name: February
tasks: Start onions, leeks, and celery seeds indoors
Prune fruit trees and grape vines while dormant
Prepare cold frames for early sowing
Spread aged compost on beds as snow melts
Test soil if possible

months.name: March
tasks: Sow peas, broad beans, and spinach outdoors as soil thaws
Start brassica seeds indoors (cabbage, kale, broccoli)
Plant parsley, chervil, and coriander seeds
Divide perennial herbs (mint, chives, thyme)
Prepare new beds with the Roman raised bed method

months.name: April
tasks: Direct sow lettuce, radish, beet, and carrot
Transplant onion and leek seedlings
Sow herbs: dill, cilantro, chamomile
Begin succession planting of salad greens
Weed diligently — Walahfrid's first spring task

months.name: May
tasks: After last frost: transplant tomatoes, peppers, squash
Plant bean and cucumber seeds directly
Set out tender herbs: basil, rosemary (in cold zones)
Mulch beds to conserve moisture (Islamic water-wise method)
Begin regular watering schedule

months.name: June
tasks: Harvest early lettuce, radishes, peas, and herbs
Succession sow beans, lettuce, and root vegetables
Gather herbs for drying at peak potency (before flowering)
Train and stake climbing plants
Watch for pests — plant aromatic companions

months.name: July
tasks: Main herb harvest for drying and preserving
Harvest garlic when lower leaves brown
Continue succession sowing for fall crops
Save seeds from best-performing plants
Water deeply in morning (Columella's advice)

months.name: August
tasks: Harvest and preserve: dry herbs, pickle vegetables, make vinegars
Sow fall crops: lettuce, spinach, radish, turnip
Plant overwintering onion sets and garlic
Begin collecting seeds for next year
Turn first compost pit into second (Roman two-pit method)

months.name: September
tasks: Plant garlic cloves for next year's harvest
Sow cover crops (clover, vetch, rye) on empty beds
Harvest root vegetables for winter storage
Dry remaining herbs
Transplant perennial herbs to permanent positions

months.name: October
tasks: Clear spent annual crops and compost the debris
Spread well-aged manure on empty beds (Columella method)
Protect tender perennials with mulch
Plant broad beans for overwintering (mild climates)
Clean and oil tools for winter storage

months.name: November
tasks: Continue composting kitchen and garden waste
Protect cold frames and cloches
Prune dormant herbs (sage, thyme, lavender)
Review garden journal and note successes and failures
Study ancient texts for next year's experiments

months.name: December
tasks: The garden rests — honor the dormant season
Read and study: Columella, Walahfrid, Tusser
Design next year's garden plan
Inventory seeds and order what's needed
Sharpen tools and mend fences

quotes.text: What reason have we for thinking that those things which the earth bears of her own accord are of no use for preserving health?
quotes.author: Cato the Elder

quotes.text: The first consideration of all for the farmer is knowledge of the soil.
quotes.author: Columella

quotes.text: From a small patch of ground, in its gifted season, Nature can bring so many kinds of fragrance and healing.
quotes.author: Walahfrid Strabo

quotes.text: In March and in April, from morning to night, in sowing and setting good housewives delight.
quotes.author: Thomas Tusser

quotes.text: No occupation is so delightful to me as the culture of the earth, and no culture comparable to that of the garden.
quotes.author: Thomas Jefferson

quotes.text: The garden is the poor man's apothecary.
quotes.author: German Proverb

quotes.text: A garden requires patient labor and attention. Plants do not grow merely to satisfy ambitions or to fulfill good intentions. They thrive because someone expended effort on them.
quotes.author: Liberty Hyde Bailey
